perturbation.original_text: 4.5 Invoicing and Payments. Converge shall pay the Monthly Installments to VNE on the dates specified in Section 4.1 and, if applicable, Section 4.2 above. All other amounts due under this Agreement will be invoiced by VNE to Converge on a monthly basis in arrears. All such invoiced amounts shall be due to VNE within 30 days following Converge's receipt of VNE's invoice. All payments will be made by Converge in U.S. dollars, without setoff, recoupment or deduction. All fees and other amounts not paid when due shall be subject to late charges of the lesser of (a) 1.5% per month of the overdue amount or (b) the maximum permitted under applicable law.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.5 Invoicing and Payments. Converge shall pay the Monthly Installments to VNE on the dates specified in Section 4.1 and, if applicable, Section 4.2 above. All other amounts due under this Agreement will be invoiced by VNE to Converge on a monthly basis in arrears. All such invoiced amounts shall be due to VNE within 30 days following Converge's receipt of VNE's invoice. All payments will be made by Converge in U.S. dollars, without setoff, recoupment or deduction. All fees and other amounts not paid when due shall be subject to late charges of 1.5% per month of the overdue amount.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'or (b) the maximum permitted under applicable law,' the contract now allows for late charges that may exceed the legal limit set by Pennsylvania law, potentially violating usury laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania law limits the amount of interest that can be charged on debts.
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania law sets a maximum interest rate, and by removing the reference to this legal limit, the contract opens itself up to charging interest or late fees that exceed that maximum, which is a violation of Pennsylvania's usury laws. Pennsylvania's legal interest rate is 6% simple interest and the maximum lawful interest rate is the greater of 6% simple interest or 5% over the Federal Reserve discount rate.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text includes a clause that limits late charges to 'the maximum permitted under applicable law.' The changed text removes this limitation, potentially allowing late charges to exceed legal limits, as explained in both the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the explanations indicate a potential violation of usury laws if the late charges exceed the legally permitted maximum in Pennsylvania. The removal of the protective clause creates a direct contradiction if the resulting late charges violate usury laws, making it a serious legal issue.

perturbation.original_text: 10.6 Choice of Law; Waiver of Jury Trial; Limitation of Action. This Agreement and performance under this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the United States of America and of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania as applied to agreements entered into and to be performed entirely within Pennsylvania between Pennsylvania residents, excluding its conflicts of law provisions. The United Nations Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods is specifically excluded from application to this Agreement. The parties expressly waive any right to a jury trial regarding disputes related to this Agreement. Unless otherwise provided by local law without the possibility of contractual waiver or limitation, any legal or other action related to this Agreement must be commenced no later than two years from the date on which the cause of action arose.
perturbation.changed_text: 10.6 Choice of Law; Waiver of Jury Trial; Limitation of Action. This Agreement and performance under this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the United States of America and of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania as applied to agreements entered into and to be performed entirely within Pennsylvania between Pennsylvania residents, excluding its conflicts of law provisions. The United Nations Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods is specifically excluded from application to this Agreement. The parties expressly waive any right to a jury trial regarding disputes related to this Agreement. Any legal or other action related to this Agreement must be commenced no later than two years from the date on which the cause of action arose.
perturbation.explanation: By removing "Unless otherwise provided by local law without the possibility of contractual waiver or limitation," the contract attempts to impose a two-year limitation on actions even where Pennsylvania law might prohibit such a short limitation or where the cause of action may take longer than two years to be discovered. This is a violation of Pennsylvania statutes of limitations and related case law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania law specifies various statutes of limitations for different causes of action. Removing the qualifier allows the contract to override these statutory limits, potentially barring legitimate claims that would otherwise be timely under Pennsylvania law, contradicting established legal protections.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 42 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5525 (2022) - Four year limitation :: 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 42 - JUDICIARY AND JUDICIAL PROCEDURE Chapter 55 - Limitation of Time Section 5525 - Four year limitation Universal Citation: 42 PA Cons Stat § 5525 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 5525. Four year limitation. (a) General rule.-- Except as provided for in subsection (b), the following actions and proceedings must be commenced within four years: (1) An action upon a contract, under seal or otherwise, for the sale, construction or furnishing of tangible personal property or fixtures. (2) Any action subject to 13 Pa.C.S. § 2725 (relating to statute of limitations in contracts for sale). (3) An action upon an express contract not founded upon an instrument in writing. (4) An action upon a contract implied in law, except an action subject to another limitation specified in this subchapter. (5) An action upon a judgment or decree of any court of the United States or of any state. (6) An action upon any official bond of a public official, officer or employee. (7) An action upon a negotiable or nonnegotiable bond, note or other similar instrument in writing. Where such an instrument is payable upon demand, the time within which an action on it must be commenced shall be computed from the later of either demand or any payment of principal of or interest on the instrument. (8) An action upon a contract, obligation or liability founded upon a writing not specified in paragraph (7), under seal or otherwise, except an action subject to another limitation specified in this subchapter.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the clause "Unless otherwise provided by local law without the possibility of contractual waiver or limitation," which directly contradicts Pennsylvania law regarding statutes of limitations. The original text acknowledges that local law may impose limitations periods that cannot be contractually waived. The changed text attempts to impose a two-year limitation on actions regardless of potentially longer periods allowed by Pennsylvania law. Scraped Law Snippet 2, 42 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5525, establishes a four-year statute of limitations for actions upon a contract. By omitting the clause, the contract now contradicts this law, making the two-year limitation potentially unenforceable and thus creating a legal contradiction, as it attempts to shorten the legally prescribed time frame.

perturbation.original_text: 5.1 Services Warranty. VNE warrants that the Services provided hereunder will be provided in accordance with generally-accepted industry standards applicable to the performance of services of a similar nature. In the event of any breach of the foregoing warranty, and provided that Converge reports such breach to VNE in writing within 90 days following the date of performance of the Services in question, VNE shall, as its sole obligation and Converge's sole and exclusive remedy, promptly repair, replace or re-perform the Services in question, without additional cost to Converge, so as to correct the warranty non-compliance as promptly as practicable (within 30 days to the extent technically feasible).
perturbation.changed_text: 5.1 Services Warranty. VNE warrants that the Services provided hereunder will be provided in accordance with generally-accepted industry standards applicable to the performance of services of a similar nature. In the event of any breach of the foregoing warranty, and provided that Converge reports such breach to VNE in writing within 30 days following the date of performance of the Services in question, VNE shall, as its sole obligation and Converge's sole and exclusive remedy, promptly repair, replace or re-perform the Services in question, without additional cost to Converge, so as to correct the warranty non-compliance as promptly as practicable (within 30 days to the extent technically feasible).
perturbation.explanation: By shortening the reporting period from 90 to 30 days, the amendment restricts the consumer's opportunity to discover and report service defects, potentially violating Pennsylvania's implied warranty laws which expect a reasonable discovery period.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania's implied warranty and reasonable discovery laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: 13 Pa.C.S.A. § 2607 requires that a buyer notify the seller of a breach within a reasonable time after they discover or should have discovered it. Reducing the reporting period to 30 days, especially for complex services where defects may not be immediately apparent, could be deemed unreasonable and violate the spirit and intent of Pennsylvania's UCC provisions regarding implied warranties and the buyer's right to adequate time for discovery of defects.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the reporting period for service breaches from 90 to 30 days. The Law Explanation states that 13 Pa.C.S.A. § 2607 requires notification of a breach within a 'reasonable time' after discovery. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this reduction could be unreasonable, particularly for complex services. Since the snippets are missing, we cannot use them. However, based on the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation the change introduces a significant risk of non-compliance by potentially violating the 'reasonable time' requirement, therefore creating a legal contradiction as defined by the Omission Legal Contradiction.
